in the Kn'crric Advice Letter differs from the analysis employed in the
Re:
Lance Olson
Olson, Hagel & Fishburn,LLp
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425
Sacramento, Ca. 95g14
Your Reqtrest for Advice
Our File No. .{-08-177
Dear Mr. Olson:
October 24,2OOB
QUESTTON
This letter responds to yoLlr request for advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (thl ..Act,,j.r 
rv6q'L
would a contract for broadcast media services between a candidate and a media
vendor count (as an expenditure) toward lre Aqs.;;;;y expenditure ceilings after a
political party assumes or pays the obligations of the canclijate under the contract?
CONCLUSION
Yes' under the circttmstances described below. a candidate makes an expencliture
that counts toward his or her voluntary e.xpenditure ceili,g when the candidate enters into
a contracr in which he or she makes oi, .rior..rur" p.o,rii"'to pay a nredia ven6or for
' The Rrlitical Retirrm '{ct is contained in Go,,ernment code .Sections ll l()(x) through glol.l. ,\ll
statut()ryrc't'erenct'saret.theG.rc'rnmentcrtle.unless.thcrwiseindiu'atecl. 
Thereguluti.ris.fthcFirir
P,liticul Prae tice's c,nrmissi.n are c()ntaincd in-se. ti,,nr.is t ri, iir,,,,si *,t997 .f Tirrc l .f the calitirrnia
[:l;,:1,]::l}lJl:l;:liJi,:'i,;J,ll,:.:':"'n'"' "" t' titre r. Divisi,,ii.fuhc carili,rni, c,,.r.,,i


File No. A-08-177
Page No. 2
broadcast air time. A subsequent payment of the candidate's contractual obligation by a
political party does not operate retroactively to change the legal consequence,-under the
Act, of the candidate's original, enforceable promise to make such a paymert.
FACTS
You write to request clarification of advice given in an advice letter published on
September 3, 2008 (Krvaric Advice Letter, No. A-08-145) concerning mattirs on which
you provide advice to clients of your law firm. The Krvaric advice letter posed the
following question:
"If a candidate who has chosen to accept a volirntary expenditure ceiling
negotiates and executes a contract for campaign advertisements with a
vendor, and the applicable disclaimers for the advertisements (if any) state
that they are paid for by the candidate, do payments on that contraci count
against the candidate's voluntary expenditure ceiling if a political party
actually pays for the advertisements with funds sent directlyio the u"ndoi,
which are at no time deposited into the candidate,s campaign account?,,
As we understood the facts stated at length in that letter, the candidate
would enter into contracts with a variety of television, cable television, and radio
stations under which the media vendors would broadcast campaign
advertisements advancing his candidacy, in return for a payment of money at a
favorable rate available only to candidates under federal law. The contracts were
between the candidate (or the candidate's agents) and the individual vendors. We
understood that the candidate would at the same time enter into side agreements
with the San Diego county Republican party, in which the party would agree to
pay the vendors at some later date, satisfying the candidate's contiactual
obligations to the vendors.
Under the facts then before us, we concluded that the party's payments to
the vendors would be non-monetary contributions which, under'Regulation
18540(c), are deemed to be campaign expenditures by the candidate, Jounting
against the candidate's voluntary expenditure ceiling.
You believe that this advice was incorrect, or at least unclear, on the
ground that "once a political party assumes or meets the obligations of a candidate
under the contract the amount of the contract could no longer count as an
'expenditure' for purposes of the voluntary expenditure limits." ?ou 
"ontend that
"[tlhis result is required by Section 85400(c) because the duty of the candidate to
pay the contract price would be extinguished as a result of the political party's
payment."


File No. A-08-177
Page No. 3
AIYALYSIS
Secrion 85400(c) provides as follows:
"(c) A campaign expenditure made by a political party on behalf of a
candidate may not be attributed to tti" timitaiions on 
"u,npoign
expenditures set forth in this section.,'
we believe it is clear that this statute applies to a transaction in which a political
party enters into a contract with a television stition under which the party promises to
pay, and actually does pay, a certain sum of money to the station, in return for which the
station promises to supply air time on which to *n advertisemenis advancinfthe
candidacy of the party's candidate.
we believe it no less clear that this statute does notapply to a transaction in which
a candidate enters into a contract with a television station under which the candidate
promises to pay, and actually does pay, acertain sum of money to the station, in return
for which the station promises to tuppty air time on whictr the candidate mayrun
advertisements advancing his o*n 
"ondidu"y.
uncertainties arise only in the kind of hybrid transaction that you and the Kvraric
advice letter describe, when the candidate enters into a binding contract ,utre.ein he or she
promises to pay a certain sum to the station for air time, after which a political party steps
forward to pay the candidate's obligation. But the argument that Section g5400(c)
springs into effect when the political party inserts itse'if into the transaction does not take
into account the legal effect of the 
"undidat"'r promise to poy the television station.
Section 82025 defines the term ,.expenditure," in pertinent part, as follows:
"Expenditure" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a
loan by a third party, or an enforceabri promise to make ct pny,rr,rt,unress
it is clear from the surrounding circumsiances that it is not ,nud. fo,
political purposes. I I An expenditure is made on the date the payment is
made or on the date consideration, if any, is received, whichevei is
earlier." (Emphasis added.)
when a candidate enters into the type of media contract described by the Kvrctric
advice letter. he or she necessarily makes a promise, enforceable under well-settled
principles of contract law' to make a payment. when the purpose of the payment is to
rttn campaign advertisements, it can scarcely be said that ii is "clear from the surrounding


File No. A-08-177
Page No.4
clrcllmstances that it is not made for political purposes." The candidate therefore makes
an "expenditure" when he or she enters into a contract made binding by mutual promises
of performance.
To the extent that the timi'ng of the candidate's expenditure may be critical to the
analysis, Section 82025 directs us to the date the poy*"ni is made or consideration is
received' whichever is earlier. As we understoodthe terms of the contracts described by
the Kvraric advice letter, they involved mutual promises by candidate and vendor - the
c.andidate will pay a price on or before a certain-date, and t-he vendor *ifi prouiae the air
time within a certain window of time.
The Act does not contain special rules for when "consideration is received.,,But
elementary principles of contract law state that, when immediate p"rro*un." is required
of neither party to a bilateral contract, consideration is receivea ana the parties are bound
when "mutuality of obligation" is established by trreir excrrange of prom'ir"r, *rri.t
becomes the considermion establishing the contiact. As explain ed in Larwin-southern
califonria, Inc. v. JGB Investrnent company, Inc. trqzgl, ror car.App. 3d626,637:
"In a bilaterar contract, the promisor and promisee must exchange
promises representing binding legal obligations to render the coitract
enforceable. The requirement thit bindiig legal obligations underlie-a
contract is generally referred to as the doctrinl of mutuality of obligation.
ln essence, mutuarity of obrigation must exist where the eichang;i 
-
promises between promisor and promisee is meant to represent the
contract's consideration."2
As we understood the arrangements underlying the question presented in the
Kvraric advice letter, they-involved precisely the kind-of bilateral contracts described
above' The contracts-would be signid, and ihe parties *Ja understand themselves
bound to their terms, lef9re ony puyrn"nt was tendered to the vendor. Thus consideration
would be received by both vendor and candidate - and the candidate would have made an
"expenditure" - before the political party took steps to intervene in the arrangement..l
Your own understanding of the timing appears to be the same, insofar as you
acknowledge an obligation of the candidat" tIuip.."ra"r uny action by the parry: ..the
duty of the candidate to pay under the contract would be extinguished as a result of the
political party's payment." Instead you contend that the,.expenditure,, made by the
candidate when consideration was exchanged on formation of the contract would be
: See also cal' civil Code Section 1605. which provides in perrinenr part that'.Any benefit
conferred' or agreed kr be conferred upon the promissor.'uy onJut# f.*on. t, which the promissor is not
lawtully entitled....is a -uood consideration fbia promise.,,
'The vultte of the "c'onsideration" to the rendor would normally be rhe dollar value of the
candidate's promise - i.e.. the contract price.


File No. A-08-177
Page No.5
"extinguished" by the party's subsequent payment. It may be correct to say that the
candidate's contractual obligation to pay the vendor is extinguished, but it does not
follow that this payment requires that what had been an "expenditure" by the candidate
must be reclassified as something else after the party pays the candidate;s obligation.
We can find no support in law, and no considerations of public policy that support
your contention that an expenditure by a political party can effectively rewrite historyio
"extinguish" an "expenditure" already made by a candidate. Section 85400(c) is a rule
goveming expenditures by a political party. It has no application in a case where the
candidate makes an "expenditure" in excess of the voluntary expenditure ceilings.
Our conclusion that an arrangement of the sort described in the Kvraric advice
letter lies beyond the scope of Section 85400(c) is premised on the essential point that,
under the facts described in that letter, the candidare would make an expendiiure subject
to the voltmtary expenditure ceiling. This statute does not purport to eximpt the
expenditures of candidates from the voluntary expenditure ceilings. It was therefore not
necessary to the analysis of the question posed by the Kvraric letter that we consider the
effect under those circumstances of a subsequent expenditure by a political party, which
is at least the kind of expenditure that is the subject to Section g5+00(c). -
We agree with you that the discussion and characterization of the party's actions
in the Kvraric letter makes the scope of Section 85400(c) less clear, and i; ,lr-""".rory.
We feel it best, therefore, that the analysis of this letter supersede any and all analysis in
the Kvraric advice letter regarding the nature and legal effect of the porty'r payment of
the candidate's legal obligation under the contract.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTwjgl
